title: What is ItsMyResearch
text: ItsMyResearch (itsmyresearch.com) is a private research workbench with 28 browser-based tools organised on the home page under the seven steps of the research process (I Define the problem · II Review the literature · III Formulate hypotheses · IV Design the research · V Collect data · VI Analyse data · VII Interpret, write & report) — each step is a collapsible section revealing its tools: Discover (Smart Literature Finder, DOI Finder & Lookup, Research Gap Identifier), Design (Research Question Generator, Survey & Interview Designer), Analyze (Qualitative Coding Assistant, Originality & AI Checker), and Write & Review (AI Peer Review, Citation Formatter, Reference Style Generator). Tools run entirely in the browser. Founded by Dr. Nagesh Sharma (Co-Founder & CEO) and Johann San Francisco (Co-Founder & CTO), with Dr. Anuja Shukla as Strategic Advisor. The team page is 'Meet the Team' at /about.

title: The Research Journey (start here for new researchers)
text: The Research Journey at /research-journey (also 'Research Journey (start here)' at the top of the Tools menu, and the compass banner on the home page) maps the entire research workflow for first-time researchers as eight plain-language steps, each with a 'You are here if…' line to self-locate, the key question the step answers, and the relevant tools linked directly under it: (1) Explore the field — Literature Finder, Bibliometric Analysis, Author & Institution Profiles, Data Source Directory; (2) Map what's known — Citation Graph, DOI Finder, My Library, Synthesis Matrix; (3) Find your gap — Gap Identifier; (4) Ask the question — Question Generator; (5) Design the study — Survey & Interview Designer, Stats Advisor; (6) Gather the data — Data Explorer and Data Source Directory for secondary data, with participant-anonymity guidance; (7) Analyze — StatPLS, Qualitative Coding, Stats Advisor; (8) Write, check, defend — Abstract & Summary Studio, Writing Polisher, Citation Formatter, Reference Style Generator, Originality Checker, RubricLens, AI Peer Review, Journal Metrics & Finder (where to submit). Keyless tools are marked, hand-off notes show how each step chains to the next, and the page ends with the reminder that looping back is normal research, not failure.

title: Research Paper Template Generator
text: The Research Paper Template Generator (the first tool on the home page) drafts a complete, structured academic paper. Pick a paper type — Literature Review (with an optional PRISMA 2020 approach: search strategy, inclusion/exclusion criteria, identification-screening-eligibility-inclusion flow), Quantitative, Qualitative, Experimental, or Theoretical — set the topic, discipline, optional research questions and key references, and a target length (short ~3,000 words, full paper ~8,000 words including references, or extended ~12,000). It writes the paper section by section with the correct structure for that type (abstract, introduction, methods/methodology, results/findings/synthesis, discussion, conclusion, references). The abstract and introduction preview FREE (one run); the rest of the sections show blurred and locked until you unlock the full paper, which costs 6 credits (or is free while billing is off). Any section that fails to generate can be regenerated with no extra credits. Output copies and downloads as Markdown and hands off to the Reference Style Generator for a formatted .docx. Strong AI disclaimer: outputs may be wrong and citations may not be real — verify every claim and reference (use the DOI Finder) before use. For a full thesis (~40,000 words), generate it chapter by chapter, treating each chapter as one paper-length run.

title: Security: how API keys and data are protected
text: Your AI provider key is stored only in your own browser's local storage on your device, and is sent only directly to the provider you chose (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, etc.) over HTTPS — it is NEVER sent to ItsMyResearch servers, so there is nothing on our side to store, log, leak, or have stolen. You can verify this in your browser's Network devtools. Other websites and other open tabs CANNOT read your key: browsers enforce the same-origin policy, which walls off each site's local storage so only itsmyresearch.com can read what itsmyresearch.com saved. Additional safeguards: no postMessage/cross-frame channels, a strict Content-Security-Policy (object-src none, frame-ancestors none, X-Frame-Options DENY), escape-everything rendering to prevent XSS, and HTTPS with HSTS. Honest limits we can't defend (true of any website): malicious browser extensions, malware or other users on your own device, and shared/public computers — for maximum safety use the free built-in browser model or a local model (Ollama/LM Studio) where no key exists and nothing leaves your device. Passwords are salted scrypt hashes; session tokens are hashed in httpOnly cookies; payments go through Razorpay and card details never reach us. Full details at /security. API keys are session-only by default: the key is kept in the browser's sessionStorage and is automatically erased when the tab closes — nothing persists between visits unless the user ticks 'Remember my key on this device' in API settings (persistent localStorage, until removed). A 'Clear key' button in API settings wipes the key from both storages instantly. On shared or public computers: leave 'Remember' off and close the tab, or press Clear key.

title: Privacy: what stays in your browser
text: Research material (papers, transcripts, manuscripts, references) pasted into tools is processed in the browser and never stored on ReWiseEd servers. AI API keys are stored only in the browser's local storage. There are no third-party analytics or ad trackers. The only cookie is a session cookie after sign-in. A first-party usage metric records which tool ran and the approximate output size — never the content. Accounts store email, name, optional profile fields, and a salted scrypt password hash. The Qualitative Coding Assistant anonymizes transcripts in the browser before any AI call (on by default): emails, phone numbers, links, ID numbers, titled names, and named speaker labels are masked, with a summary of what was masked; the original text never changes. Masking is best-effort — researchers must still follow their ethics protocol.

title: RubricLens — rubric alignment checker
text: RubricLens matches a draft against its assignment rubric before submission — 'see your work through your grader's eyes'. Load the document and the rubric each by pasting text or uploading .docx, .pdf, .txt, or .md (files are parsed entirely in the browser and never uploaded; PDF reading is best-effort and scanned PDFs may need copy-paste). Pick a grader persona — supportive coach, balanced grader, or strict examiner — and run. Output: a summary table of band/points per criterion, a criterion-by-criterion verdict citing the rubric's own descriptors with short verbatim evidence quotes from the draft, what each criterion needs to reach the top band, and the three highest-impact fixes. Uses your chosen AI provider (Claude, OpenAI, Gemini, or the free built-in model). It is a revision aid with an explicit caveat that it estimates a reading, not the actual grade. Hands off to the Writing Polisher.

title: AI providers: bring your own key, or none at all
text: AI-powered tools use the provider chosen in API settings on any tool page. Options: Anthropic Claude (key from console.anthropic.com, default model claude-sonnet-5), OpenAI, Google Gemini (key from aistudio.google.com/apikey, default gemini-2.5-flash), OpenRouter, Groq, Together AI (your own API key, sent only in direct browser-to-provider requests); free local models via Ollama or LM Studio (no key); or the free Built-in browser model (no key at all) — it downloads a ~2 GB open model once from Hugging Face's CDN, caches it on your device, and runs via WebGPU in Chrome or Edge, so your content never leaves your machine. A Test connection button verifies any setup. Provider costs, where they exist, are billed by the provider, not ReWiseEd.

title: Which tools work without any API key
text: Fully keyless tools: Smart Literature Finder (search), DOI Finder & Lookup, and the stylometric and scholarly-overlap sections of the Originality & AI Checker, plus the Reference Style Generator's document mechanics (all six styles). Also fully keyless: Bibliometric Analysis (field mapping) and Journal Metrics & Finder (where to submit). They use the free OpenAlex and Crossref scholarly indexes. Tools that need an AI key (or a free local model): Research Gap Identifier, Research Question Generator, Survey & Interview Designer, Qualitative Coding Assistant, AI Peer Review, Citation Formatter, the AI rerank in the Literature Finder, the AI-writing assessment in the Originality Checker, and the citation cleanup pass in the Reference Style Generator.

title: Smart Literature Finder
text: Searches 240M+ scholarly works through the free OpenAlex index without any API key. Features: year range filters, open-access-only filter, sorting by relevance, citations, or recency, abstracts, citation counts, open-access PDF links, BibTeX export of all results, and an optional AI relevance rerank using your key. The 'Find the gaps' button sends results straight into the Research Gap Identifier.

title: DOI Finder & Lookup
text: Two keyless directions. DOI to paper: paste a DOI in any form (bare, https://doi.org/ URL, or doi: prefix) to get the full record — authors, venue, year, citations, abstract, open-access PDF link — with copy citation and BibTeX export. Reference to DOI: paste messy references one per line; each is matched against Crossref and OpenAlex, re-ranked to prefer original papers over derivative records, and labelled with a match-confidence badge (strong / likely / weak — verify). Bulk copy of all DOIs and a combined BibTeX export.

title: Statistical Data Explorer
text: Keyless tool for official statistics. Search the World Bank's 1,500+ World Development Indicators by keyword (GDP, consumption, inflation, education, health, trade), pick up to 8 countries and a year range, then get a comparison table, an SVG trend chart, a CSV export with source provenance headers, and an APA 7 dataset citation with retrieval date. Results can be saved to My Library. Requests route through ItsMyResearch's caching proxy because the World Bank API is often slow (observed 1.5-60 seconds); cached queries return instantly and only public statistics pass through — never user content. Missing values show as '—' meaning the World Bank reports no value for that country-year, not zero; recent years are frequently incomplete and series are revised retroactively.

title: Abstract & Summary Studio
text: Upload a thesis or dissertation (.docx, .pdf, .txt, .md — parsed entirely in the browser, never uploaded) or paste text, pick output types, and generate: an APA 7 abstract (150-250 words, one paragraph, with a Keywords line), an executive summary for practitioners (~300/600/900 words with The problem / What was done / What was found / What it means / Recommended actions), a conference abstract capped at 250 words, and a plain-language summary at grade-8 reading level. For long documents the Studio automatically prioritizes conclusion, discussion, findings, and introduction sections and says what was skimmed. Grounding rules: only what the document states — missing elements are marked [not stated in document], never invented. Each output streams into its own card with copy and download, plus a combined download. Uses your chosen AI provider or the free built-in model.

title: Data Source Directory
text: A curated guide to official statistics sources with what each covers, coverage years, and access notes: World Bank Open Data (in-app), World Bank Global Consumption Database, IMF Data, Eurostat, OECD Data Explorer, UN Data, Statistics Canada, Asian Development Bank, JETRO, and U.S. Federal Statistics (Data.gov, FRED, Census, BLS, BEA). Every source is publicly accessible and free to reuse under open or public-domain terms — none require a paid subscription or an institutional login. Sources are labelled 'in-app' when data can be pulled inside ReWiseEd or 'portal' when the source has no usable open API and a good link is the honest answer. Filterable by keyword and topic tag. Includes how to cite a dataset in APA 7 with attribution.

title: StatPLS (PLS-SEM)
text: StatPLS is keyless, fully in-browser partial least squares structural equation modeling (PLS-SEM), implementing the published PLS-SEM literature (Wold, Lohmoller, Henseler and colleagues) with a workflow familiar to SmartPLS users: upload a numeric CSV of survey responses, define latent constructs with reflective indicators (Mode A), draw structural paths, and run the Lohmoller PLS algorithm with path, factor, or centroid weighting. Model building is fast: an Auto-build button groups columns by prefix (att1, att2 → ATT), a TAM template creates the classic Perceived Ease of Use / Perceived Usefulness / Attitude / Behavioral Intention model with items matched by prefix, chips can be clicked or DRAGGED between construct cards, and an indicator may sit in multiple constructs (repeated-indicators higher-order modeling). Outputs: outer loadings (0.708 threshold), Cronbach's alpha computed from the actual item correlations, Dijkstra-Henseler rho_A, composite reliability, AVE, outer weights, cross-loadings with item-level discriminant flags, Fornell-Larcker, HTMT, bootstrapped path coefficients (default 5,000 resamples per the PLS-SEM reporting literature, or 500-10,000 custom) with t, p and percentile CIs, f-squared, inner VIF, total effects (direct + indirect), R-squared and adjusted, a DRAGGABLE SVG path diagram (drag constructs, arrows and betas follow), an APA-style write-up, and downloadable report and latent scores. A built-in full TAM example (n=250, four attitude items) demonstrates everything. Limits: reflective measurement only, recursive models only, no moderation; results can differ slightly from SmartPLS 4; independent tool, not affiliated with SmartPLS GmbH. An optional AI interpretation (your key or the free built-in model) reads the full results and writes a discussion: which hypotheses are supported, effect magnitudes, quality concerns, and what to check next. The diagram uses standard SEM notation: construct circles with R-squared inside, indicator boxes with loadings on the arrows, all draggable. StatPLS is a multi-method workbench: alongside PLS-SEM it offers PROCESS-style analyses on observed scale scores — Mediation (Hayes Model 4: a, b, c', c paths and a percentile-bootstrapped indirect effect with 1,000-5,000 resamples, labelled full/partial mediation) and Moderation (Hayes Model 1: mean-centered interaction, delta R-squared, simple slopes at mean plus/minus 1 SD) — and classic multiple regression (B, SE, standardized beta, t, p, VIF, R-squared, F). Items assigned to a role are averaged into a scale score. Honestly not included: CB-SEM (needs ML covariance fitting — use lavaan or AMOS), GSCA, and PROCESS models beyond 1 and 4. A Methods & attribution section maps every statistic to its canonical academic source (Wold 1982; Lohmoller 1989; Efron 1979; Cronbach 1951; Fornell & Larcker 1981; Dijkstra & Henseler 2015; Henseler, Ringle & Sarstedt 2015; Cohen 1988; Hayes 2022; Aiken & West 1991; Davis 1989 for TAM; Hair et al. 2022 for reporting practice) with a one-click 'Copy citations (APA)' button producing all 12 full references with DOIs for the user's methods chapter. Exporting results: every result table has CSV download and 'Copy table' (pastes as a real table into Word/Excel); the path diagram exports as PNG (2x resolution), SVG (vector, scales cleanly in Word/LaTeX), or copies to the clipboard as an image — via buttons under the diagram or by right-clicking the diagram itself. Works in every mode (StatPLS, mediation/moderation, regression). 'All tables (.xlsx)' downloads ONE Excel workbook with every result on its own tab (Contents, Loadings, Reliability, Outer weights, Cross-loadings, Fornell-Larcker, HTMT, Paths with full bootstrap statistics, Effects direct/indirect/total, R2, Latent scores) — the Contents sheet links to every tab and each tab's A1 cell links back to Contents. Generated fully in-browser with no library; opens in Excel, Numbers, LibreOffice, and Google Sheets. Measurement model table: StatPLS renders the reflective measurement model in the exact journal/publication layout — construct name and its Cronbach's alpha, rho_A, CR (rho_c), and AVE each SPAN (merged cells) across that construct's indicator rows, with per-indicator loadings alongside, threshold-coloured green (meets) or amber (below). This is the ready-to-paste table reviewers expect. It exports intact: CSV/Copy fill the merged values down each row, the Excel workbook has a dedicated 'Measurement model' tab, and the .md report + PDF include it. Assessment guidance follows the established 5-step structural workflow (collinearity/VIF → path significance → R² at 0.75/0.50/0.25 per Hair, Ringle & Sarstedt 2011 → f² effect sizes → predictive relevance, with Q²/blindfolding honestly noted as not yet computed), as compiled in the SmartPLS assessment guide by Dr. Anuja Shukla, ItsMyResearch's Strategic Advisor. The methods card and copyable reference list now carry 18 canonical citations (adding Bagozzi & Yi 1988; Chin 1998; Hair et al. 2010; Hair, Ringle & Sarstedt 2011; Henseler, Ringle & Sinkovics 2009; Nunnally & Bernstein 1994). The diagram has an 'Expand canvas' toggle: fit-to-window by default, or a full-size scrollable canvas for drag-arranging the layout. Unconnected constructs are allowed: if a construct has no structural path, StatPLS still generates its full measurement model (loadings, α, ρA, CR, AVE, cross-loadings) and notes that it carries no path coefficients — the run no longer errors on isolated constructs.

title: Literature Synthesis Matrix
text: Paste a set of papers (titles and abstracts — the Literature Finder's 'Build the matrix' button sends results directly) and, using your AI key or the built-in model, get a structured comparison matrix: one row per paper across aims, methods, samples, findings, and limitations (or methods-, findings-, or theory-focused column sets), with '—' for anything the abstracts don't state. Below the table: a synthesis of where studies agree, where they conflict with likely explanations, visible trends, and a strength-of-evidence paragraph. Hands off to the Gap Identifier.

title: Academic Writing Polisher
text: Improves your own academic prose without changing its substance, using your AI key or the built-in model. Four tasks: polish academic tone, tighten clarity and concision, generate a structured abstract (150-250 words plus keywords, marking anything the draft doesn't report rather than inventing it), or suggest 6-8 title options across styles. Polish and clarity modes list every substantive edit pattern so you stay the author; the tool never adds claims or citations you didn't write and never removes a citation. American or British English. Hands off to the Reference Style Generator.

title: Author & Institution Profiles
text: Keyless tool to look up any researcher or institution via OpenAlex. Search by name, pick the right profile from matches (names aren't unique — affiliation and ORCID disambiguate), and see works count, total citations, h-index, i10-index, 2-year mean citedness, affiliations, and the six most-cited papers with save-to-library and map-citations links. Institution mode shows country, type, homepage, and top output. Metrics are OpenAlex's and typically run lower than Google Scholar because coverage differs; recent and non-English work is less complete.

title: Related papers ('papers like this')
text: The DOI Finder and Citation Graph include a 'Papers like this' panel powered by OpenAlex related_works and shared topics — a quick way to branch out from one paper to similar work, each result linking to its DOI, a citation map, and save-to-library. It reflects OpenAlex's similarity model and is a starting point, not a systematic search; coverage is thinner for very recent, non-English, and non-journal outputs.

title: Library recommendations ('more like my library')
text: In My Library, the 'More like my library' button derives the dominant topics across your saved papers (those with DOIs) and returns the most-cited OpenAlex papers in those topics that you don't already have. Topic-based discovery ranked by citations — a discovery aid, not a systematic search — with save-to-library on each suggestion.

title: Citation Graph Explorer
text: Keyless tool that maps a paper's citation network using OpenAlex. Enter a DOI, OpenAlex ID, or title and see a visual graph: the works it builds on (its references) on the left, the paper in the centre, and newer research citing it on the right, each node showing first author, year, and citation count. Click any node to re-centre the graph on that paper and keep exploring. The centre paper can be saved to your library, and the whole visible set can be sent to the Literature Synthesis Matrix.

title: My Library (saved papers)
text: A personal reference library for signed-in users. Save papers with the Save button on Smart Literature Finder results, DOI Finder lookups, and the Citation Graph Explorer; they collect at /library (also in the Tools menu) with title, authors, year, venue, and DOI. Each paper takes free-text notes, the whole library exports as BibTeX, and 'Analyze in pipeline' sends the collection with your notes into the Literature Synthesis Matrix. Duplicate DOIs are detected, the limit is 1000 papers, and deleting your account deletes the library with it.

title: Research Gap Identifier
text: Paste a literature set (titles and abstracts, or a review draft). Using your AI key it identifies unexplored research gaps grouped by theme, contradictory findings, methodological weaknesses, underrepresented populations and contexts, and concrete follow-up research questions, citing the input papers. Output streams live and downloads as Markdown. One click sends the gaps onward to the Research Question Generator.

title: Research Question Generator
text: Turns a topic plus constraints into precise, testable research questions. Each question includes a variables table — independent variable (IV), dependent variable (DV), and control variables with operationalisation naming real instruments where standard ones exist — plus directional H1 and null H0 hypotheses, a suggested study design with its key validity threat, and a feasibility note honoring stated constraints. Controls for study design type and question count. Ends with a where-to-start recommendation and can hand off to the Survey & Interview Designer.

title: Survey & Interview Designer
text: Turns research questions into data-collection instruments. Survey mode: sections by construct, explicit response scales, preference for validated instruments (e.g. PSQI, GAD-7) with licensing flags, attention checks, a design-notes table, and pilot and validity notes. Interview mode: opening script, core questions ordered broad-to-specific with probes, sensitive-topic handling, and interviewer notes. Length control targets roughly 5 or 10 minutes, or in-depth. Any validated instrument the designer names comes with a References section in APA 7 — canonical sources only, with [verify — check via DOI Finder] flags instead of guessed details — and generated instruments include an explicit participant anonymity/confidentiality assurance in the consent preamble.

title: Statistical Test Advisor
text: Describe your study, variables, and hypotheses (output from the Question Generator or Instrument Designer drops straight in) and, using your AI key, get per-question recommendations: the right statistical test matched to your variable types, assumptions with how to check each, the non-parametric or robust fallback, the effect size to report with thresholds, a sample-size note, a fill-in-the-blanks APA 7 results sentence, and starter code for R, Python, or SPSS. Ends with pre-analysis reminders about pre-registration, missing data, and multiple comparisons.

title: Qualitative Coding Assistant
text: Performs a Braun & Clarke (2006) reflexive thematic analysis on interview transcripts using your AI key: familiarisation notes, an open-codes table with verbatim quotes, candidate themes with descriptions, a thematic map narrative, and an analyst reflexivity note. Grounded only in the transcript — it does not invent quotes. Separate multiple transcripts with a divider line. The page carries a methods attribution with the full Braun & Clarke (2006) citation and DOI for the user's methods chapter, plus a reminder that AI-assisted coding must be reviewed by the researcher.

title: Originality & AI Checker
text: The Originality & AI Checker's identity: 'the checker that shows its work' — unlike single-score checkers (QuillBot, Turnitin AI detection), it never gives one opaque percentage. Five sections: (1) instant stylometric signals (burstiness, vocabulary variety, repeated 8-word passages, stock AI phrases); (2) a sentence-level evidence heatmap — every flagged sentence is highlighted with a hover tooltip naming exactly which signal families fired and why, across three independent families (Rhythm: sentence-length cadence; Vocabulary: lexical variety and surprise; Phrasing: stock AI phrases) with an honest agreement verdict — when the families disagree the tool says 'inconclusive' rather than inventing confidence; (3) free rule-based revision guidance for the strongest-flagged sentences — the concrete edit that removes each signal; (4) keyless scholarly-overlap scan of the most distinctive passages against OpenAlex's 240M works with linked sources; (5) optional AI forensic assessment using your key with quoted evidence. Everything runs in the browser — the document is never uploaded (stylometry and heatmap are fully local; overlap sends only short passages to the scholarly index). A downloadable evidence report covers all sections with a limitations statement. Explicitly honest that no AI detector is reliable and results must never drive high-stakes decisions alone. NEW — Paraphrase & Humanize studio, built into the same page: six rewrite modes (Natural voice/humanize, Academic polish, Clear & simple, Formal, Concise, Creative variation), three strength levels, and a citation-safety option that reproduces quotes and citations exactly. Unlimited length (QuillBot's free tier caps at 125 words), no account, all modes free with your own key, fully private. After rewriting: word-by-word change view, an 'Explain the edits' summary, and one-click 'Re-check this rewrite' so users loop check → fix → re-check in one place. You can also rewrite only the flagged sentences from the originality report. Integrity stance stated in the UI: a writing aid, not a disguise — it does not make text 'undetectable', and institutional AI-disclosure rules still apply. The report opens with an at-a-glance dashboard showing TWO SEPARATE headline scores with donut gauges: AI-writing likelihood % (who wrote it — signal-based estimate from rhythm/vocabulary/phrasing, labeled Low/Uncertain/High, explicitly 'evidence not proof') and Source similarity % (does it match published work — weighted overlap of scanned passages against OpenAlex, with matched-source count). The UI explains they are independent: a human essay can plagiarize; an AI text can be 0% similar.

title: Peer Review Simulator
text: Simulates a journal peer review of a pasted manuscript using your AI key: summary of contribution, strengths, numbered major concerns each with why it matters and a concrete fix, minor concerns, reproducibility notes, and a recommendation (Accept to Reject). Reviewer temperament is selectable: constructive, rigorous, or adversarial worst-case Reviewer 2. Can hand the manuscript on to the APA 7 Formatter.

title: Citation Formatter
text: Two modes. Precise (keyless, recommended): each reference is matched to its DOI and rendered by Crossref's official citation formatter in APA 7, MLA 9, Chicago author-date, Harvard, IEEE, or Vancouver — exact and never invented; references without a DOI are flagged rather than guessed, and uncertain matches carry a verify-match badge. AI format (your key): handles references without DOIs such as books, reports, and web pages, flagging missing details as [missing].

title: Reference Style Generator (formerly APA 7 Paper Formatter)
text: The Reference Style Generator formats a whole paper as a downloadable Word (.docx) document in any of six reference styles: APA 7, MLA 9, Chicago 17 (author-date), Harvard, Vancouver/ICMJE, and IEEE. Upload a .docx (parsed fully in the browser — never uploaded to a server) or paste text with # heading markers. Each style gets its true mechanics: APA 7 title page (student or professional with running head) and hanging-indent References; MLA 9 first-page heading block, 'Lastname 1' page header, centered title, and Works Cited; Chicago title page with no page number on it and author-date References; Harvard Reference List; Vancouver references numbered 1. 2. 3. in citation order; IEEE bracket-numbered [1] references (manuscript format — IEEE journals typeset two-column at publication). All are Times New Roman 12, double-spaced, 1-inch margins, automatic page numbers. The optional AI pass (any provider, or the free built-in model) rewrites in-text citations and the reference list to match the chosen style exactly — (Author, year) for APA/Harvard, (Author page) for MLA, (Author year) for Chicago, [1] numerals for Vancouver and IEEE — marking missing details as [missing: ...] rather than inventing them. Without a key you still get all document mechanics. Reached at /apa-formatter (also /reference-style-generator). A third APA paper type, 'Thesis / dissertation', follows standard university thesis templates: dissertation title page (By / Submitted in partial satisfaction / degree and program / institution — every field editable, with placeholders like [University Name] filling anything left blank), committee chair and co-chair approval page with signature lines, a live Table of Contents field (right-click, Update Field in Word), ALL-CAPS running head left-aligned with the page number right on every page, every chapter and appendix starting on a new page (chapters labeled 'Chapter N. Title'), APA Level 3 headings in bold italic, an Abstract with an indented Keywords line, and reference URLs rendered as live blue underlined hyperlinks for accessibility.

title: Cross-tool hand-offs (the pipeline)
text: Tools chain with one click: Literature Finder results feed the Gap Identifier ('Find the gaps'), gap analysis feeds the Research Question Generator, generated questions feed the Survey & Interview Designer, and the AI Peer Review can send your manuscript to the APA 7 Formatter. Hand-offs carry the work through browser storage — nothing is uploaded.

title: Accounts and signing up
text: Accounts are optional — every tool works without one. Signing up requires an email, a password of at least 8 characters, solving a drag-to-match puzzle, and accepting the Terms and Privacy Policy. A confirmation email from noreply@rewiseed.com must be clicked before first sign-in. Profiles support a photo, school/university, LinkedIn, Twitter/X, and an about section, editable from the profile page reached via the avatar in the navigation.

title: Roles: Student, Admin, Super Admin
text: Three roles. Students are regular accounts. Admins manage students of their own institution (matched by the School/University profile field): enable/disable accounts and set per-tool access. Super Admins manage everything: all users, role promotion between student and admin, institutions, deletion, and platform-wide analytics. The Admin console is at /admin, reachable via the shield Admin button in the navigation that appears only for admin and super admin accounts.

title: Per-tool access control
text: All tools require a free account: signed-out visitors can browse the whole site (home, Research Journey, pricing, the JBMS journal) but must sign in to open any tool — this keeps work private and metered to each user. Admins and super admins can grant specific tools to a user via the Tools button on each admin-console row (with Basic/Student/All presets), and can select multiple users to assign toolsets in bulk. How users discover their access: on the home page, tools outside their level show a LOCK badge (🔒 LOCKED) with a See-how-to-unlock link; opening a locked tool shows a page stating their current level and which level includes that tool, with buttons to the pricing page (Who-gets-what table) and home. Restricted users also see only granted tools in the navigation menu. Anonymous (signed-out) visitors currently retain public access to all tools. Admins can also grant or deduct run credits for any of their metered users (basic/student/educator) via the Credits button on each admin-console row — quick +25/+50/+100 buttons or a custom amount (negative to deduct); balances are capped at 300, staff are never metered, and every adjustment is logged for audit without counting as revenue.

title: Usage analytics for admins
text: The admin console shows a 30-day dashboard: total tool runs, estimated AI tokens, accounts, active-this-week, new signups, anonymous runs, and a per-tool breakdown. Token figures are estimates from output length (about 4 characters per token) because BYOK requests go directly from the browser to the AI provider — exact provider token counts are not visible. Institution admins see only their own institution's aggregate.

title: Passwords: change, forgot, reset
text: Change password from the profile page's Security card (requires the current password; other devices are signed out). Forgot password: use the link on the sign-in page — a one-time reset link valid for 1 hour is emailed from noreply@rewiseed.com; completing it also confirms the account. All sessions are revoked on reset.

title: Deleting an account
text: Users can permanently delete their own account from the Danger zone card on the profile page, confirmed with their password. Deletion removes the account, profile, photo, sessions, and tokens. Super admins can delete any non-superadmin account from the admin console. The last remaining super admin cannot delete themselves.

title: The drag-to-match puzzle
text: Sign-up, sign-in, and password-reset requests are protected by a drag-to-match puzzle: drag each symbol onto its name, or tap a symbol then tap its name on touch screens. It is verified server-side, single-attempt per challenge, and tokens are one-time and expire in five minutes.

title: Pricing
text: Pricing (at /pricing): every tool run consumes run credits, and every tool page shows its cost next to your balance before you click. Standard (non-AI) runs — a literature search, DOI lookup, journal-rankings comparison, citation-graph exploration, bibliometric analysis, author-impact analysis, scholar-profile search, data-explorer query, citation-integrity scan, citation-formatter batch, or StatPLS model run — cost a flat 1 credit. AI generations cost 1 credit up to 20k characters of input, 2 up to 80k, 4 up to 200k. Journal Rankings & Comparison is completely free and unlimited; browsing, reading, filtering, the Library, and the JBMS journal are also free, as are exports of results you already generated. Sign-in is required to run tools. Free monthly allowances by level: Basic 5 welcome credits at signup (one-time), Students 25/month, Educators/Researchers 50/month — refreshed every 30 days by topping the balance back up to the allowance (never stacked; paid credits above the allowance are untouched). ItsMyResearch Pro is Rs 499/month for 150 credits (stack on the free allowance, roll over to 300); a Rs 199 top-up adds 25. Every new confirmed account gets one free AI run. Admin and super-admin (staff) accounts are never metered. When a metered account runs out of credits, ItsMyResearch shows a top-up pop-up and (at most once every 30 days) emails a reminder with the top-up and Pro options. The Citation Integrity Checker costs 5 credits per manuscript scan (it does deeper work than a standard 1-credit run).

title: Terms of Service and legal
text: The Terms of Service cover eligibility (13+), acceptable use including academic integrity responsibility, a prohibition on using originality signals punitively against people, BYOK key responsibility, AS-IS warranty disclaimer, and liability limits. AI outputs may be incorrect and must be verified before relying on them — they are not professional advice. Terms and Privacy Policy acceptance is required at signup and recorded with version and timestamp.

title: Contact and support
text: ItsMyResearch is at itsmyresearch.com. Transactional email comes from noreply@rewiseed.com. The 'Meet the Team' page (/about) lists the founders, Dr. Nagesh Sharma and Johann San Francisco, and Strategic Advisor Dr. Anuja Shukla, with their LinkedIn profiles. For account issues, use the password reset flow, or contact your institution's admin if your account is managed.

title: Platform intro tour
text: First-time signed-in users see a short cinematic intro tour of the platform on the home page. It can be skipped with Esc, advanced by clicking or arrow keys, and replayed anytime from any page via the Tools menu's 'Platform intro' entry, or the 'Replay the intro' link in the home page footer.

title: Offline and air-gapped use
text: The tool pages are self-contained HTML/CSS/JS with no external asset dependencies. Pointing the API settings at a local model (Ollama or LM Studio) makes AI tools run with zero external traffic. The keyless scholarly tools need internet access to reach OpenAlex and Crossref. Accounts and the assistant need the hosted server.

title: Bibliometric Analysis — map a whole research field
text: The Bibliometric Analysis tool maps an entire research field from one query: total publications and growth curve per year, most prolific authors, top journals and venues, leading institutions, output by country, open-access share, and the most-cited anchor papers. It runs live against OpenAlex (240M+ works), is keyless (1 credit per analysis), and exports every table as CSV. Filters: year range and document type (articles, reviews, books). It also generates a ready-to-paste methods sentence with APA references (Donthu et al., 2021 for the bibliometric workflow; Priem et al., 2022 for OpenAlex). An optional 'AI reading of the field' interprets the aggregates with your own AI key — only summary counts are sent, never raw records; that AI step uses a run credit, the data itself is free. It also cross-checks the same field in other databases: PubMed is queried live (keyless, via NCBI E-utilities) with the count, its share of the OpenAlex total, an interpretation (biomedical footprint or not), and the most relevant PubMed papers; Google Scholar and ScienceDirect have no open APIs, so the tool hands you the exact same search there in one click with honest notes (Scholar counts run broadest; ScienceDirect needs institutional access). Honest limits: OpenAlex favors English, DOI-bearing journal work; author counts rely on automated name disambiguation; recent years undercount.

title: Journal Metrics & Finder — decide where to submit
text: The Journal Metrics & Finder helps you choose where to submit a paper. 'Find journals for my paper' ranks venues by how many articles and reviews they publish on your topic — a fit signal, deliberately sorted above impact. 'Look up a journal' (by name or ISSN) shows its profile: 2-year mean citedness (OpenAlex's open impact-factor-like measure), journal h-index, works and citations, articles-per-year trend, publisher, open-access status, DOAJ listing, and listed APC. You can compare up to four journals side by side. Keyless via OpenAlex — 1 credit per search. Important honesty: the 2-yr mean citedness is NOT the official Journal Impact Factor (Clarivate) or SJR (Scimago) — those are proprietary; each profile links the journal's Scimago page and website so you can verify. The tool also warns about predatory journals and links Think. Check. Submit. This is our SciMago-style feature built on open data.

title: Verify citations — automatic hallucination check on AI outputs
text: Every AI tool output has a 'Verify citations' button, and the Research Paper Template Generator has one for the whole draft. It cross-checks the output against Crossref, the publishers' own registry: every DOI is resolved (a DOI that doesn't exist is flagged as 'failed — likely fabricated'), and DOI-less reference lines are bibliographic-searched and marked verified only on a strong title match. Results show the registry record next to each reference with a link. Important honesty: 'unverified' does not prove a reference is fake — books, chapters, and non-DOI works are often absent from Crossref — the tool says so and tells users to confirm manually via Google Scholar or their library. Every AI output also carries a standing disclaimer that the user must verify claims, numbers, and citations and meet their institution's integrity rules. The Citation Formatter's keyless mode and DOI Finder do deeper per-reference verification. Every AI output, the StatPLS results, the Originality report, and the Paper Generator draft can also be exported as PDF — the Export PDF button opens the browser's print dialog (choose 'Save as PDF') with a clean document layout; generation is fully in-browser.

title: Journal Rankings & Comparison — ABS, ABDC, quartiles side by side
text: The Journal Rankings & Comparison tool answers 'where does this journal stand?' for 3,188 business & management journals, instantly and keyless. For each journal it shows five things side by side: FT50 membership (the Financial Times' 50-journal list used for its business-school research rank, 2026 revision (added Academy of Management Annals, American Sociological Review, Psychological Science; removed Human Relations, Journal of Business Ethics, Organization Studies) — all 50 flagged, with an FT50 filter chip and a comparison row), the ABS/AJG 2024 rating (4* to 1, © Chartered Association of Business Schools), the ABDC 2025 rating (A* to C, © Australian Business Deans Council), the Scopus-based best quartile (Q1–Q4, from the SCImago Journal Rank 2025 release (the current edition)), and impact metrics (2-yr cites/doc as the open impact-factor analogue, SJR, H-index — all SCImago 2025 — plus a LIVE OpenAlex 2-yr citedness in the comparison view for a current reading). Search by title, ISSN, or publisher; filter by field (22 ABS fields) and by rating chips (ABS 4*/4/3/2/1, ABDC A*/A/B/C, Q1–Q4 — filters combine); sort by best-ranked, either list, or SJR. Compare up to 5 journals side by side with best-in-row highlighting, then export the comparison as CSV or PDF. Honesty built in: ratings belong to their publishing bodies, quartiles are a dated snapshot with Scimago links for current values, the official Clarivate JIF is proprietary and not shown, and users are told to confirm on the official list their institution uses. For journals outside business/management, the Journal Metrics & Finder covers all of scholarship via OpenAlex. No impact factor is shown by design — the official JIF is Clarivate's proprietary metric and unofficial approximations invite confusion; the tool reports prestige rank (quartile, SJR, H-index), and citation counts live in the Journal Metrics & Finder.

title: Citation Integrity Checker — every in-text citation ↔ reference list
text: The Citation Integrity Checker does the check reviewers do first: every in-text citation must appear in the reference list, and every reference must be cited in the text. Upload a .docx/.pdf/.txt (parsed fully in-browser — the manuscript never leaves the device) or paste the text including the References section. It parses APA-style citations — parenthetical, narrative, and multi-reference groups like (A, 2020; B et al., 2021) — and reports per citation: matched (author+year), author mismatch (with the closest entry shown), year mismatch, or missing reference; plus the reverse list of references never cited. APA 7 style warnings included: ambiguity when two references share a lead surname (use initials/year suffixes), 'and' vs '&' in narrative vs parenthetical citations. Summary dashboard with issue counts, matched citations hidden by default (toggle to show), export as PDF report or Markdown. Keyless — 1 credit per manuscript scan. Limits stated honestly: numeric/Vancouver styles not supported, unusual formats can mis-parse, 'as cited in' may flag falsely — it's a first pass, verify each flag; cross-check reference details with the DOI Finder.

title: Who can create an account — institutional-email access policy
text: Anyone can create an account. The email determines the starting tier: institutional emails (.edu, .edu.<cc>, .ac.<cc> like mit.edu/iitb.ac.in/ox.ac.uk, or rewiseed.com, or an allow-listed address) start as 'student' with the curated essentials toolset; all other emails (Gmail, Yahoo, Outlook, company addresses) can still join but start on the limited 'basic' tier with only the Smart Literature Finder and DOI Finder & Lookup. Admins can raise anyone's tier (basic → student → educator) or grant specific extra tools per user. The gate is no longer a block — non-institutional users simply have limited default access on the basic tier. Levels also carry free monthly AI credits: Basic 5 welcome credits (one-time), Student 25/month, Educator 50/month, refreshed every 30 days by topping the balance up to the allowance. Locked tools are visible on the home page with a lock badge so every user can see what a higher level or Pro unlocks. Note: the tools themselves now require sign-in for everyone — anonymous visitors can read the site but cannot open a tool.

title: User roles & feature access — Student, Educator/Researcher, Admin
text: There are five account levels: Basic (non-institutional signups — only Literature Finder + DOI Finder by default), plus, each with different feature (tool) access. Student: a curated essentials set only — Literature Finder, DOI Finder, Citation Formatter, Reference Style Generator, Writing Polisher, Originality & AI Checker, Citation Integrity Checker, RubricLens, Research Question Generator, Statistical Test Advisor, Research Gap Identifier, Author Impact Dashboard, and Journal Rankings & Comparison. Educator/Researcher: full access to every tool (StatPLS, Bibliometrics, Journal Rankings, Paper Generator, etc.). Admin: full access plus user management. Each level also carries a free monthly AI-credit allowance: Basic 5 welcome credits one-time, Student 25/month, Educator 50/month (Pro subscription adds 150/month on top for any level). Admin and super-admin accounts are never metered. Super admin: full platform control. New signups default to Student. Admins manage users on the Admin console: change a person's level (student <-> educator; super admins can also make admins), and grant or restrict individual tools per user with an explicit override (Student set / All tools presets, or tick specific tools). The override wins over the role default; leaving it at the role default means the account auto-gets newly added tools. Access is enforced server-side — restricted tool pages return 'Tool not enabled' — and the nav menu shows only the tools a user can open. Admins see and manage only students and educators of their own institution; super admins see everyone. Admins can assign tools two ways: per user (the 'Tools' button on a user row opens a tick-list with Student-set / All-tools presets and Save), or in BULK — tick the checkbox on several users (or the header select-all), then 'Assign tools to selected' opens one tool picker whose choice is applied to every selected user at once. Both replace each account's tool access and are enforced server-side; the bulk action respects the same guardrails (never superadmins/yourself, org-scoped for institutional admins).

title: AI Peer Review — journal-grade manuscript review
text: AI Peer Review (formerly the Peer Review Simulator) gives your manuscript a journal-grade review before a human sees it. It scores six criteria the way real reviewers do — originality & novelty, theoretical/practical contribution, methodological rigour, clarity & structure, literature grounding, and ethics & integrity — with a scorecard table, strengths, numbered major and minor concerns each with a concrete fix, an ethics/reproducibility section, and a recommendation (accept / minor / major / reject). Choose reviewer temperament (constructive, rigorous, adversarial). It is decision-support for authors, never a substitute for human peer review, and it hands off to submitting to the journal. Uses your own AI key. Old URL /peer-review-simulator redirects here.

title: Journal of Business, Management & Sustainability (JBMS) — submit, review, publish
text: JBMS is ItsMyResearch's own peer-reviewed, open-access, monthly journal for multidisciplinary research across business, management, entrepreneurship, sustainability, and related fields (11 tracks). It runs a real editorial pipeline: authors submit at /journal-submit (title, abstract, keywords, track, authors, full manuscript by upload or paste, cover letter, required integrity self-checks, and COPE-aligned declarations on originality, concurrent submission, ethics, conflicts, AI use, and authorship); a handling editor desk-screens; at least two independent reviewers score six criteria double-anonymously (reviewers see the manuscript but not author identities); a human editor decides accept/minor/major/reject; accepted papers are published to the monthly issue under CC-BY. The editorial desk at /journal-desk adapts by role — authors track submissions and see reviews, invited reviewers complete structured review forms, and editors (admin/superadmin) screen, invite reviewers by email, record decisions, and publish. Published articles are open access with citation + PDF export. Ethics follow COPE core practices, CRediT authorship, and DOAJ transparency; corrections/retractions and an appeals process are defined. Fees: currently free to publish — no submission fee and no article processing charge while the journal is established; open access under CC-BY. Any future charge would be announced transparently, apply only on acceptance, come with waivers, and never influence a decision. Honest posture: it is a new journal building its record; indexing (DOAJ, Scopus) and DOI registration are pursued over time and never claimed prematurely. Editors = admin/superadmin roles; the editorial board is placeholder until real academics are appointed. DOIs: every published article gets a persistent DOI. Real resolvable DOIs are minted via Crossref (2026: ~US$200/yr small-publisher membership + ~US$1/article); until the journal registers a Crossref prefix (set via the CROSSREF_DOI_PREFIX env var), each article carries a reserved DOI suffix (e.g. jbms.v1i7.0001) that becomes a live https://doi.org link once the prefix is active. A free bridge is depositing accepted articles to Zenodo for a real DataCite DOI. Editors can paste a registered DOI onto any published article from the editorial desk. The journal never claims a DOI/index it doesn't hold.

title: Author Impact Dashboard — fair, normalised author analytics
text: The Author Impact Dashboard gives a deep, co-authorship-normalised view of any researcher's publication footprint, keyless via OpenAlex (1 credit per analysis) — a fairer alternative to raw-count author metrics. Search a name, pick the profile (ORCID + affiliations shown), and if one person has several OpenAlex records you can tick and MERGE them into one deduplicated view. Metrics: works, cites/paper, weighted cites/paper (each paper's citations divided by its number of authors), fractional works (sum of 1/authors — shared credit), distinct co-authors and per paper, corresponding-author count and %, and an author-position breakdown (first/second/third/fourth+). Tabs: Snapshot (metrics + position + top fields + publications-per-year chart), Articles (sortable table, click any column), Venues, Topics (% of works), Geography (countries + institutions from your affiliations), Collaborators (top co-authors with joint-work counts, each linkable to their own dashboard), and Integrity (OpenAlex retraction flags with per-paper list + PubPeer link). Export CSV or PDF. Honest limits stated in-tool: normalisation formulas explained; retraction flags are prompts not verdicts; deeper integrity screens (Problematic Paper Screener, Scopus delisting) need external sources; up to 300 works fetched; coverage is OpenAlex's. Deep-linkable via ?a=<openalex-author-id> or ?q=name. Complements the simpler Author & Institution Profiles lookup. Independent tool, not affiliated with other author-metrics sites.

title: Feedback button — send the team a note
text: Every page has a Feedback button (fixed, bottom-left). Clicking it opens a simple form — a message and an optional reply email. On submit it emails the ItsMyResearch team directly and shows a 'Thank you for providing your valuable feedback' confirmation. Anyone can use it, signed in or not; the recipient addresses are server-side only and never exposed to the browser. Rate-limited to prevent abuse.

title: Navigating by research step — breadcrumb and grouped Tools menu
text: Two ways to move between tools and their research step: (1) every tool page shows a breadcrumb chip under the top nav — e.g. '← Step VI · Analyse the data' — that jumps back to that exact step on the home page (the step opens and scrolls into view); (2) the Tools dropdown in the nav is grouped by the seven steps with sticky step headers that stay pinned while you scroll the menu — each header is itself a link to that step on the home page. The menu is scrollable (max ~72vh), shows the current tool highlighted, and for restricted accounts hides ungranted tools and any step group that ends up empty.

title: Paper Q&A
text: Paper Q&A (/paper-qa) lets you load your own papers — upload PDF/DOCX/TXT or paste — and ask them questions in a chat. Answers are grounded strictly in the sources you provide, cite the source number (e.g. S1) and quote the supporting text, and say so plainly when something is not in the sources. Documents are read in your browser and never uploaded to ItsMyResearch; the AI runs on your own key (BYOK). It sits in Step II (Review the literature). Each question costs 1–4 AI credits by how much source text is in context. It is our privacy-first, integrity-first answer to chat-with-PDF tools — built independently, using only your own uploaded text.

title: Data Q&A
text: Data Q&A (/data-qa) lets you upload a CSV/TSV dataset and ask questions in plain language. Summary statistics (mean, sd, quartiles, missing), pairwise Pearson correlations, and quick charts (histogram, scatter, category-mean bar) are computed exactly in your browser — so numbers are accurate and your data never leaves your device — and the AI interprets them grounded in those computed figures. It complements StatPLS: hand off to StatPLS for confirmatory PLS-SEM modelling. It sits in Step VI (Analyse the data). Each question costs 1–4 AI credits by data size. Built independently as a private, locally-computed alternative to cloud data-analysis chatbots.
